title: UK Mini-Marts Caught Selling Cocaine and Cannabis in BBC Investigation
summary: A BBC investigation using secret filming has revealed that mini-marts on UK High Streets are openly selling illegal drugs, including cocaine, cannabis, laughing gas, and prescription pills. Undercover researchers easily purchased cannabis and cocaine from a shop in Cradley Heath, West Midlands. The shopkeeper offered 'weed, coke, everything' and obtained cocaine within hours. The National Crime Agency (NCA) and Chartered Trading Standards Institute (CTSI) state that organized criminal gangs are exploiting shopfronts to push drugs. Analysis of local news reports found drugs in over 70 shops across the UK, from Devon to Northern Ireland. A senior Labour MP, Liam Byrne, is calling for urgent government action, saying 'We can't restore our High Streets unless we take out the cancer of organised crime.' The Home Office says it is working with police and agencies to take strong action. Legitimate businesses in affected areas report intimidation by gangs and witnessed knife and gun violence.

keywords.explanation: a secret inquiry where researchers pretend to be customers to gather evidence
criminal activities planned and controlled by powerful groups
medicines that can only be obtained with a doctor's order
a UK agency that fights serious and organized crime
frightening or threatening someone to make them do what you want

options: Chair of the Business and Trade Committee
questions.correct_answer: Chair of the Business and Trade Committee

background_read: Organized crime involves groups of people who work together to commit serious crimes for profit. These groups often use legitimate businesses as fronts for illegal activities. The UK has agencies like the NCA and Trading Standards to combat such crimes. High Streets are important for local communities, and crime can harm businesses and residents.
Article_Structure: The article exposes that mini-marts on UK High Streets are selling illegal drugs, with undercover researchers easily purchasing cannabis and cocaine. The main points include the widespread nature of the problem across the UK, the involvement of organized criminal gangs, and the intimidation of legitimate businesses. The purpose is to raise public awareness and pressure the government to act. Evidence includes secret footage, test purchases, and analysis of local news reports. The BBC is a credible source with a track record of investigative journalism. Methodology involved undercover research and filming over a year-long investigation.

perspectives.description: Police and NCA acknowledge the problem and are working to take action against criminal businesses.
Home Office says it is working with police and agencies to take strong action.
Legitimate businesses report intimidation and violence from gangs.
